text: The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg with the table tilted left. Robotic arms are mounted to trocars, and instruments are inserted. The surgeon identifies the ileocolic pedicle by retracting the ileocolic junction anteriorly and laterally. The peritoneum over the pedicle is incised, isolating the ileocolic artery and vein, which are clipped and transected using high ligation.

The surgeon performs mesenteric dissection using a medial to lateral approach, staying between the embryological planes anterior to Toldt's and Gerota's fascia and the duodenum. The hepatic flexure is dissected medially, and the dissection extends to the root of the middle colic vessels, where the middle colic artery and vein are clipped and transected.

The surgeon applies caudal traction on the transverse colon to enter the bursa omentalis, dividing the gastrocolic tissue from right to left up to the splenic flexure. Utilizing the robotic arms' multi-quadrant access, the dissection is redirected to the right lower quadrant. Medial traction is applied to separate the lateral attachments of the right colon. The terminal ileum is prepared and transected using an endoscopic linear stapler.

The robotic system is repositioned, rotating from the right to the left side of the patient for the dissection of the left colon and rectum. The operating table is tilted fifteen degrees to the right. The small bowel loops are retracted out of the pelvis into the right upper quadrant. The sigmoid colon is retracted anteriorly, and the peritoneum is incised at the promontorium level, entering the aorta mesenteric window. The inferior mesenteric artery pedicle is identified, dissected, clipped, and transected while preserving the nerve plexus. The inferior mesenteric vein is divided similarly.

The surgeon dissects the mesentery of the left colon in a medial-to-lateral fashion, carefully preserving the left ureter, ornatal vessels, and autonomic nerves. The left colon is then retracted medially, and its lateral attachments are divided. The splenic flexure is taken down, completing the colonic dissection before directing the robotic dissection towards the pelvis.

The surgeon performs total mesorectal excision by dissecting the rectum, beginning with the incision of the lateral peritoneal reflection below the sacral promontory. The rectum is mobilized posteriorly from the sacral hollow using sharp dissection between the fascia propria recti and presacral fascia. The posterior mesorectum is dissected first, facilitating the lateral dissection. The right and left lateral sides of the mesorectum are mobilized, followed by dissection on the anterior plane until reaching the anorectal ring.
The surgeon adjusts the angle of the robotic camera from a downward to upward position to visualize the intersynthetic area, revealing the levator ani muscles.

The surgeon transects the rectum using an articulating endoscopic linear stapler introduced through the suprapubic trocars. The specimen is extracted through an extended suprapubic incision. A J-pouch is constructed with a linear stapler, and an anvil of the circular stapler is placed at the distal end of the pouch. After re-establishing pneumoperitoneum, the circular stapler is introduced trans-anally to perform the pouch-anal anastomosis, ensuring the intestine is not twisted and the vagina is not interrupted in the staple line. The procedure concludes with the creation of a diverting loop ileostomy.
